annotations.label: Jordan normal form

anchor.snippet: is an upper triangular matrix of a particular form called a Jordan matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Jordan matrix or Jordan normal form definition (no `jordanBlock`/normal-form constructions).

annotations.label: Existence condition for Jordan form

anchor.snippet: a basis with respect to which the matrix has the required form exists if and only if all eigenvalues of the matrix lie in K

annotations.note: Generalized eigenspaces span the space over an algebraically closed field, but the existence of a Jordan basis under the splitting condition is not stated.

annotations.label: Algebraic multiplicity

anchor.snippet: the number of times each eigenvalue occurs is called the algebraic multiplicity of the eigenvalue

annotations.note: Algebraic multiplicity exists only as `charpoly.rootMultiplicity` (= dim of the maximal generalized eigenspace), with no dedicated named definition.

annotations.label: Non-uniqueness of Jordan form

anchor.snippet: the normal form for a given M is not entirely unique

annotations.note: No Jordan normal form exists in Mathlib, so its non-uniqueness up to block order is not stated.

annotations.label: Diagonal form as special case

anchor.snippet: The diagonal form for diagonalizable matrices, for instance normal matrices , is a special case of the Jordan normal form.

annotations.note: Without a Jordan normal form, the statement that diagonalization is a special case of it is absent.

annotations.label: Diagonalizability criterion

anchor.snippet: is diagonalizable if and only if the sum of the dimensions of the eigenspaces is n

annotations.note: Mathlib uses semisimplicity (eigenspaces spanning the space) rather than a `Diagonalizable` predicate, so the eigenspace-dimension criterion is only implicit.

annotations.label: Defective matrix

anchor.snippet: matrices that are not diagonalizable are called defective matrices

annotations.note: There is no `defective` predicate (nor a `Diagonalizable` predicate to negate) in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-diagonalizable matrix example

anchor.snippet: Including multiplicity, the eigenvalues of A are

annotations.note: This is a worked numerical example, not a general statement formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Similarity to block diagonal Jordan matrix

anchor.snippet: In general, a square complex matrix A is similar to a block diagonal matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib has the generalized-eigenspace (primary) decomposition over an algebraically closed field, but not similarity to a block-diagonal Jordan matrix.

annotations.label: Jordan normal form of A

anchor.snippet: J is called the Jordan normal form of A

annotations.note: No definition of the Jordan normal form of a matrix exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jordan block

anchor.snippet: Each J i is called a Jordan block of A

annotations.note: There is no `jordanBlock` definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eigenvalues are diagonal entries

anchor.snippet: Counting multiplicities, the eigenvalues of J , and therefore of A , are the diagonal entries

annotations.note: Only the diagonal-matrix case (eigenvalues are the diagonal entries) is formalized; the triangular/Jordan version is not.

annotations.label: Geometric multiplicity

anchor.snippet: its geometric multiplicity is the dimension of ker( A − λ i I )

annotations.note: Geometric multiplicity is the finrank of the eigenspace (named in this lemma's docstring) but has no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Algebraic multiplicity as sum of block sizes

anchor.snippet: The sum of the sizes of all Jordan blocks corresponding to an eigenvalue

annotations.note: Algebraic multiplicity equals the dimension of the generalized eigenspace (this lemma), but the Jordan-block-sum description is absent.

annotations.label: Diagonalizable iff multiplicities coincide

anchor.snippet: for every eigenvalue λ of A , its geometric and algebraic multiplicities coincide

annotations.note: Semisimplicity is characterized by generalized eigenspaces collapsing to eigenspaces, but the explicit geometric=algebraic multiplicity criterion is not stated.

annotations.label: Jordan block as λI + N

anchor.snippet: The Jordan block corresponding to λ is of the form λI + N , where N is a nilpotent matrix

annotations.note: No Jordan block exists; the related Jordan-Chevalley splitting acts on the whole operator, not per block.

annotations.label: Number of Jordan blocks of size at least j

anchor.snippet: The number of Jordan blocks corresponding to λ i of size at least j is

annotations.note: Counting Jordan blocks by size requires Jordan structure not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiplicity in minimal polynomial

anchor.snippet: its multiplicity in the minimal polynomial is the size of its largest Jordan block

annotations.note: The relation between minpoly multiplicity and largest Jordan block size is not formalized.

annotations.label: Jordan form via similarity transformation

anchor.snippet: The Jordan normal form is obtained by some similarity transformation

annotations.note: This is a worked example of computing a Jordan form, which Mathlib does not support.

annotations.label: Generalized eigenvectors

anchor.snippet: are called generalized eigenvectors of A

annotations.note: Generalized eigenspaces `genEigenspace`/`maxGenEigenspace` are defined, whose nonzero members are the generalized eigenvectors.

annotations.label: Obtaining the normal form

anchor.snippet: This example shows how to calculate the Jordan normal form of a given matrix.

annotations.note: A computational example of producing a Jordan form, not formalizable without the construction.

annotations.label: Jordan chain

anchor.snippet: every corresponding Jordan block gives rise to a Jordan chain of linearly independent vectors

annotations.note: Jordan chains of generalized eigenvectors are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generator / lead vector

anchor.snippet: The generator , or lead vector , p b of the chain is a generalized eigenvector

annotations.note: No notion of a Jordan-chain generator/lead vector exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jordan form equivalent to basis of chains

anchor.snippet: is equivalent to the claim that the underlying vector space has a basis composed of Jordan chains

annotations.note: The equivalence of Jordan form with a basis of Jordan chains is not formalized.

annotations.label: Existence of Jordan normal form (proof)

anchor.snippet: any complex-valued square matrix A may be put in Jordan normal form

annotations.note: The generalized-eigenspace decomposition over an algebraically closed field is present, but full Jordan-form existence is not proved.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of Jordan form

anchor.snippet: the Jordan normal form of a given matrix A is unique up to the order of the Jordan blocks

annotations.note: Without a Jordan normal form construction, its uniqueness up to block order is not stated.

annotations.label: Index of an eigenvalue

anchor.snippet: is also called the index of λ

annotations.note: `maxGenEigenspaceIndex` is the least k at which the generalized eigenspace stabilizes, i.e. the index of the eigenvalue.

annotations.label: Real Jordan form

anchor.snippet: there exists a real invertible matrix P such that

annotations.note: The real Jordan normal form is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Real Jordan block

anchor.snippet: A real Jordan block is either identical to a complex Jordan block

annotations.note: Real Jordan blocks are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jordan–Chevalley decomposition

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a field
anchor.snippet: any M can be written as a sum D + N where D is semisimple

annotations.note: This proves an endomorphism over a perfect field is a sum of commuting nilpotent and semisimple parts, both polynomials in it.

annotations.label: Elementary divisors (modules)

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a field
anchor.snippet: the polynomials ( x − λ ) k are the elementary divisors of M

annotations.note: The structure theorem produces prime-power quotient factors `R⧸(pᵉ)`, but they are not named or packaged as elementary divisors.

annotations.label: Jordan form as corollary of structure theorem

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a field
anchor.snippet: the structure theorem for finitely generated modules over a principal ideal domain

annotations.note: The structure theorem for f.g. modules over a PID is formalized, but the derivation of Jordan form from it is not.

annotations.label: Spectral mapping theorem

anchor.snippet: then for any polynomial p , p ( A ) has eigenvalues

annotations.note: The spectral mapping theorem for polynomials is proved for elements of algebras over an algebraically closed field, generalizing the matrix statement.

annotations.label: Similar matrices share characteristic polynomial

anchor.snippet: Similar matrices have the same characteristic polynomial

annotations.note: Conjugation by a unit preserves the characteristic polynomial (also `LinearEquiv.charpoly_conj`).

annotations.label: Cayley–Hamilton theorem

anchor.snippet: every matrix A satisfies its characteristic equation

annotations.note: Cayley-Hamilton is proved as `aeval f f.charpoly = 0` (and `Matrix.aeval_self_charpoly`).

annotations.label: Minimal polynomial

anchor.snippet: The minimal polynomial P of a square matrix A is the unique monic polynomial of least degree

annotations.note: `minpoly` defines the monic least-degree annihilating polynomial for any algebra element, including matrices/endomorphisms.

annotations.label: Degree of minimal polynomial

anchor.snippet: the minimal polynomial of A has degree

annotations.note: The Jordan-based formula for the minimal polynomial's degree (sum of largest block sizes) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Elementary divisors of a matrix

anchor.snippet: The elementary divisors of a square matrix A are the characteristic polynomials of its Jordan blocks

annotations.note: Elementary divisors as characteristic polynomials of Jordan blocks are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear elementary divisors imply diagonalizable

anchor.snippet: If all elementary divisors are linear, A is diagonalizable

annotations.note: A squarefree (hence over an algebraically closed field, distinct-linear-factor) minimal polynomial gives semisimplicity, Mathlib's analog of diagonalizability.

annotations.label: Invariant subspace decomposition

anchor.snippet: Every Jordan block J i corresponds to an invariant subspace X i

annotations.note: The decomposition into invariant generalized eigenspaces is formalized, but the finer per-Jordan-block invariant subspaces are not.

annotations.label: Index of eigenvalue (decomposition)

anchor.snippet: is called the index of λ i and denoted by

annotations.note: `maxGenEigenspaceIndex` is exactly the index of the eigenvalue (least exponent stabilizing the generalized eigenspace).

annotations.label: Spectral projection

anchor.snippet: is called the spectral projection of A at v i

annotations.note: Spectral projections onto generalized eigenspaces of a general operator are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spectral projections mutually orthogonal

anchor.snippet: Spectral projections are mutually orthogonal in the sense that

annotations.note: Mutual orthogonality of spectral projections is not formalized since these projections are not defined.

annotations.label: Spectral theorem for normal operators

anchor.snippet: the subspaces X i 's in the first decomposition are one-dimensional and mutually orthogonal

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the spectral theorem for self-adjoint/Hermitian operators (an orthonormal eigenbasis), a special case of the normal-operator statement.

annotations.label: Index of a complex number

anchor.snippet: its index can be defined as the least non-negative integer ν ( λ ) such that

annotations.note: `maxGenEigenspaceIndex` is the least ν at which ker(A−λ)^ν reaches the maximal generalized eigenspace.

annotations.label: Conjugacy classes not affine subspaces

anchor.snippet: do not constitute linear or affine subspaces in the ambient matrix spaces

annotations.note: No statement about matrix conjugacy classes failing to be affine subspaces exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arnold's flat normal form problem

anchor.snippet: Find a canonical form of matrices over a field for which the set of representatives of matrix conjugacy classes is a union of affine linear subspaces

annotations.note: Arnold's flat normal form problem is not addressed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Daugulis solution for algebraically closed fields

anchor.snippet: It was solved for algebraically closed fields by Peteris Daugulis.

annotations.note: Daugulis's flat normal form construction is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Function applied to a Jordan block

anchor.snippet: Applying the function on a n × n Jordan block J with eigenvalue λ results in an upper triangular matrix

annotations.note: The formula for a holomorphic function applied to a Jordan block is not formalized.

annotations.label: Power function on Jordan block

anchor.snippet: The following example shows the application to the power function

annotations.note: The explicit power of a Jordan block is a worked example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jordan form for compact operators

anchor.snippet: A result analogous to the Jordan normal form holds for compact operators on a Banach space

annotations.note: A Jordan-form analog for compact operators is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spectral points of compact operators are eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: every point x in the spectrum of a compact operator T is an eigenvalue

annotations.note: For a compact operator the nonzero spectrum points are exactly the nonzero eigenvalues (via the Fredholm alternative).

annotations.label: Holomorphic functional calculus

anchor.snippet: The holomorphic functional calculus is defined as follows

annotations.note: Mathlib has the continuous functional calculus but no holomorphic (Riesz-Dunford) functional calculus.

annotations.label: Properties of functional calculus

anchor.snippet: Φ extends the polynomial functional calculus

annotations.note: Properties of the holomorphic functional calculus are not formalized since it is not defined.

annotations.label: e_i(T) is a projection

anchor.snippet: By property 3 of the functional calculus, the operator

annotations.note: The idempotency of holomorphic-functional-calculus spectral idempotents is not formalized.

annotations.label: Identification of spectral projections

anchor.snippet: Each e i ( T ) is the projection onto the subspace spanned by the Jordan chains corresponding to λ i

annotations.note: Identifying functional-calculus idempotents with projections onto generalized eigenspaces is not formalized.

annotations.label: Explicit functional calculus for matrices

anchor.snippet: gives an explicit form of holomorphic functional calculus for matrices

annotations.note: An explicit holomorphic functional calculus for matrices is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pole of an operator

anchor.snippet: The point λ is called a pole of operator T with order ν if the resolvent function

annotations.note: Mathlib has `resolvent`/`resolventSet` but no definition of a pole of an operator with an order.

annotations.label: Order of eigenvalue equals index

anchor.snippet: the order of an eigenvalue coincides with its index

annotations.note: The coincidence of pole order with eigenvalue index is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sensitivity of Jordan form to perturbations

anchor.snippet: its Jordan normal form is very sensitive to perturbations

annotations.note: Numerical-stability remarks about the Jordan form are not formalizable content in Mathlib.
